food_subgroup: Whole fish, steamed or prepared whole

ontology.foodex2_description: Sea bass (Lates calcarifer / Asian seabass / barramundi)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 226766009 refers to sea bass generically; used as closest parent for local Asian seabass (Lates calcarifer).

serving.description: 1 whole fish (700–800 g live weight, approximately 500–560 g edible portion after steaming)
serving.natural_language_portion: One whole local seabass of 700–800 g live weight yields approximately 530 g of edible steamed fish flesh as a shared main dish for 2–4 persons.

nutrient_source.title: Barramundi, raw (Lates calcarifer) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 15209; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo 'Steamed Fish' entry and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for seabass
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central data for raw barramundi (Lates calcarifer), adjusted upward for protein concentration and downward for water content to reflect steamed preparation (approximately 10–15% moisture loss). Sodium value reflects light soy sauce dressing typical of Chinese-style steamed fish preparation, estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo steamed fish reference. Core macros are consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA values for Lates calcarifer.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of protein-dominant fish foods — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed local seabass. GI is estimated at 5 based on the established principle that protein-dominant, virtually carbohydrate-free fish foods have a negligible glycemic response. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for other lean fish (e.g., canned tuna, GI ~0; fish fillet, GI ~0–10). Glycemic load per 530 g serving calculated as (0.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 530 g / 100) × 5 / 100 = 0.13, rounded to 0.1.

hypertension.guidance: Steamed seabass is a lean, high-protein fish with moderate sodium (~310 mg per 100 g) due to the light soy sauce dressing; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or consume smaller portions to limit sodium intake to within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed local seabass contains minimal carbohydrate (~0.5 g per 100 g) and has a negligible glycemic index, making it an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes with virtually no impact on postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed local seabass provides lean protein and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (~1.3 g per 100 g) with low saturated fat (~1.0 g per 100 g), supporting cardiovascular health; the soy sauce sodium content should be monitored in patients with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Local Seabass (金目鲈, Jin Mu Lu) is a classic Chinese-style whole fish dish popular in Singapore, typically prepared using a live or fresh local seabass (Lates calcarifer, also known as barramundi or siakap in Malay) weighing 700–800 g. The fish is steamed whole over high heat for 8–12 minutes until the flesh is just cooked through, then dressed with a mixture of light soy sauce, a small amount of sesame oil, and finished with a pour of hot oil over a garnish of julienned ginger and spring onion. This technique produces moist, tender, delicately flavoured white flesh with a clean, mild sweetness characteristic of freshwater-raised barramundi. The dish is a centrepiece of Singaporean seafood restaurant menus and is commonly ordered at zi char (煮炒) stalls and seafood restaurants such as The Seafood Market. Nutritionally, steamed seabass is a high-protein, low-fat food providing approximately 20 g of protein and 4.2 g of fat per 100 g, with beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and vitamin B12. Its carbohydrate content is negligible (~0.5 g per 100 g), giving it a very low glycemic index suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from the soy sauce dressing (~310 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally halal and dairy-free. It is typically shared among 2–4 diners as part of a multi-dish Chinese meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in steamed seabass?
Is steamed fish good for diabetics?
Is barramundi the same as seabass?
Calories in whole steamed fish Singapore
meal_context: Shared dish in multi-course Chinese meal

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central raw barramundi data (Lates calcarifer) with adjustments for steaming preparation and soy sauce sodium contribution, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo steamed fish entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA data. No exact HPB entry for steamed whole local seabass was found; adaptation is well-supported but not an exact match. GI is an analogous estimate based on established fish GI principles from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of edible steamed fish flesh including soy sauce dressing. Raw barramundi USDA values were adjusted for steaming (approximately 10–15% moisture loss concentrating protein and fat) and sodium was increased to reflect typical light soy sauce dressing used in Chinese-style steamed fish. 2. The serving size of 530 g represents the approximate edible yield from a 700–800 g whole live fish after steaming and bone removal. 3. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g as fish contains no dietary fibre; this is naturally zero for all fish flesh. 4. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg as fish flesh contains no meaningful vitamin C; this is naturally zero for cooked fish. 5. Trans fat is 0.0 g; confirmed zero for unprocessed fish flesh per USDA FoodData Central. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central barramundi data; no -1 sentinel values are used as reasonable estimates are available from the approved source. 7. GI is set to 5 as an analogous estimate for a protein-dominant, near-zero-carbohydrate fish food, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database fish entries. 8. The cuisine tag 'Crab' in the input is not applicable to this record and has been disregarded; this record is for seabass only. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_03411433 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as exact matches for Lates calcarifer (barramundi/Asian seabass). SNOMED CT 226766009 is used as closest_parent as it refers to sea bass generically.
